text:
The mechanism of cleavage of carbon–halogen bonds in copper-catalysed cross-coupling reactions has been the subject of much speculation over the years due to the instability of the proposed high-valent copper species and the ambiguities inherent in the redox chemistry of copper. Here we present a detailed investigation into the preparation and structural elucidation of stable alkyl Cu(III) species obtained by oxidative addition of α-haloacetonitriles to Cu(I) salts, both ionic and neutral. Stable alkyl Cu(III) species are made stable by coordination of strong-field ligands (trifluoromethyl) which hinder reductive elimination.

We recently communicated1 that cuprate reactivity can be controlled to undergo $S_N2$-type $trans$-effect assisted $S_N2$ substitution vs oxidative addition involving a halogen atom transfer (XAT) mechanism by changing the copper source from ionic to neutral neutral with a ligand environment that includes two five-membered chelating rings, i.e. bipyridine (2,2'-bipy). The neutral complex exhibits substantial ligand acceleration, as seen with other combinations of alkyl electrophiles and nucleophiles with copper catalysts. With these findings at hand, we are in a position to confirm the Cu(I)/Cu(III) redox manifolds in cross coupling reactions of alkyl electrophiles and to learn how to create high reactivity at the metal center.
